# Scorched-Earth Erdős Sweep — Turn 90

This is turn 90. Turns 1 through 89 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

CRITICAL — use ONLY the files attached to THIS message. Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_history…md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 90.

## Turn 90 — the terminal scalar branch is a canonical pullback

The candidate remains unchanged:

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

The tame relation is implicit. In the proof, `40491355905` denotes the genuine profinite idempotent \(\omega _2\); the displayed integer is its verifier residue modulo \(85667662080\). 

Turn 89 reduced the proof to fixed tame frames and used a terminal scalar theorem when every chief factor of the wild kernel is trivial. The missing detail is that this reduction is not merely a counting analogy: the target itself is canonically a pullback from its tame quotient and a finite \(2\)-group.

### Terminal scalar pullback theorem

Let \(G\) be finite, let \(L\triangleleft G\) be a \(2\)-group, and put
\[
H=G/L.
\]
Assume \(H\) is a finite tame quotient and every \(G\)-chief factor contained in \(L\) is trivial.

Let \(H_2\) be the maximal \(2\)-quotient of \(H\), and set
\[
N=\ker(H\longrightarrow H_2).
\]

Then:

\[
|N|\ \text{is odd};
\]

there is a unique normal subgroup \(\widetilde N\triangleleft G\) mapping isomorphically onto \(N\);

\[
[\widetilde N,L]=1;
\]

the quotient
\[
Q=G/\widetilde N
\]
is a finite \(2\)-group; and there is a canonical isomorphism
\[
\boxed{G\cong H\times_{H_2}Q.}
\tag{90.1}
\]

#### 1. The kernel \(N\) is odd

Write the finite tame quotient as
\[
H=\langle s,t\mid t^s=t^2,\ t^e=s^n=1\rangle
   \cong C_e\rtimes C_n.
\]

Conjugation preserves the order of \(t\), while \(t\mapsto t^2\), so \(e\) is odd. Writing
\[
n=2^a r,\qquad r\ \text{odd},
\]
the maximal \(2\)-quotient is \(H_2\cong C_{2^a}\), and
\[
N=\langle t,s^{2^a}\rangle
\]
has order \(er\), hence odd.

#### 2. The odd lift centralizes the whole scalar tower

Let \(P\) be the inverse image of \(N\) in \(G\). By Schur–Zassenhaus, \(P\) has an odd-order complement
\[
\widetilde N\cong N.
\]

Choose a \(G\)-chief series
\[
1=L_0\triangleleft L_1\triangleleft\cdots\triangleleft L_m=L.
\]
Every factor \(L_i/L_{i-1}\) is trivial, so
\[
[L_i,\widetilde N]\le L_{i-1}.
\]
Consequently
\[
[L,{}_m\widetilde N]=1.
\]

Since \(\widetilde N\) acts coprimely on the \(2\)-group \(L\),
\[
[L,\widetilde N]=[L,\widetilde N,\widetilde N]
               =[L,{}_m\widetilde N]
               =1.
\tag{90.2}
\]

Thus
\[
P=L\times\widetilde N.
\]

All complements to \(L\) in \(P\) are \(L\)-conjugate, but \(L\) centralizes \(\widetilde N\). Hence the complement is unique. Since \(N\) is characteristic in \(H\), \(P\triangleleft G\); uniqueness makes \(\widetilde N\) characteristic in \(P\), and therefore
\[
\widetilde N\triangleleft G.
\]

#### 3. The pullback description

Set \(Q=G/\widetilde N\). Its order is
\[
|Q|=\frac{|G|}{|N|}=|L|\,|H_2|,
\]
so \(Q\) is a \(2\)-group. Moreover,
\[
Q/L\cong H/N=H_2.
\]

The two quotient maps define
\[
\iota:G\longrightarrow H\times_{H_2}Q,
\qquad
g\longmapsto(gL,g\widetilde N).
\]
Its kernel is
\[
L\cap\widetilde N=1.
\]
Also,
\[
\left|H\times_{H_2}Q\right|
   =\frac{|H||Q|}{|H_2|}
   =|G|.
\]
Therefore \(\iota\) is an isomorphism, proving (90.1).

### Exact fixed-frame counting consequence

Fix a tame frame
\[
\alpha:T_{\mathrm{tame}}\twoheadrightarrow H
\]
and a scalar decoration
\[
\theta:G\to E,
\]
where \(E\) is elementary abelian.

Because \(\widetilde N\) has odd order, \(\theta\) kills \(\widetilde N\), so it factors through a map
\[
\bar\theta:Q\to E.
\]

For either source
\[
\Gamma\in\{\Gamma_A,G_{\mathbb Q_2}\},
\]
a homomorphism \(\Gamma\to G\) inducing \(\alpha\) is, under (90.1), precisely a homomorphism
\[
\beta:\Gamma^{(2)}\to Q
\]
satisfying
\[
Q\longrightarrow H_2
\quad\circ\quad\beta
=
\alpha_2:\Gamma^{(2)}\to H_2.
\tag{90.3}
\]

The decoration condition is simply
\[
\bar\theta\beta=\psi.
\tag{90.4}
\]

The marking is automatic: the wild subgroup has trivial \(H\)-coordinate, and (90.3) forces its \(Q\)-coordinate into
\[
\ker(Q\to H_2)=L.
\]

Most importantly, no assertion of the form “surjective projections imply surjectivity onto the fiber product” is needed. Generation is retained as the literal tuple condition
\[
\left\langle
(\alpha(\sigma),\beta(\sigma)),
(\alpha(\tau),1),
(1,\beta(x_0)),
(1,\beta(x_1))
\right\rangle
=
H\times_{H_2}Q.
\tag{90.5}
\]
Thus there is no hidden Goursat-type step.

The candidate and the local group have the same generator-marked maximal pro-\(2\) quotient
\[
\left\langle
\sigma,x_0,x_1
\ \middle|\
x_0^2x_1^3x_1^\sigma=1
\right\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}\text{-}2},
\]
with scalar cup/Bockstein form
\[
((a,b,c),(a',b',c'))
\longmapsto ac'+ca'+bb'.
\]
Hence the sets of \(\beta\) satisfying (90.3)–(90.5) are identical for the two sources. 

Therefore, for every fixed tame frame and every scalar decoration,
\[
N_{\Gamma_A}(G,L;\alpha,\theta,\psi)
=
N_{G_{\mathbb Q_2}}(G,L;\alpha,\theta,\psi)
\]
whenever all wild chief factors are scalar.

This is the uniform structural statement: every terminal cyclic, quaternionic, or deeper scalar Frattini tower is literally a pullback from the common \(2\)-primary boundary. There is no independent higher Bockstein constant left to determine in this branch.

**ESTABLISHED:** Proved the canonical terminal-scalar pullback theorem \(G\cong H\times_{H_2}Q\). The odd complement is unique, normal, and centralizes the entire scalar chief tower. Fixed-frame decorated surjection counts consequently reduce exactly to the common maximal pro-\(2\) quotient, with generation retained explicitly and no hidden subdirect-product assumption.

**GAP:** The private 5402-group verifier remains unrun. A publication-grade note still needs a precise bibliographic citation for ordinary Thompson criticality and polished consolidation of the finite-Heisenberg and Wall–Arf/local-Kummer lemmas.

**NEXT:** Insert this theorem as the complete terminal branch of the Turn-89 fixed-frame induction, append the unchanged residue block, and submit that exact block to the official verifier.

[BREAKTHROUGH]
